scenario: Two process instances are waiting on a Receive Task with message name OrderPaid and correlation key =orderId. A message is published with orderId = "ORD-100". Both waiting instances have orderId = "ORD-100".
question: What happens?

options.text: Both instances receiving the message simultaneously would mean one published message correlates to multiple waiting subscriptions for the same process definition. Messages are not broadcast to all matching same-process subscriptions the way BPMN Signals are.

Both messages being rejected implies the broker would discard a message when multiple same-process subscriptions match the same correlation key. This is also mistaken. Zeebe does not reject the message; it buffers it and correlates it to one matching subscription, leaving the other waiting for a subsequent publish. This characteristic distinguishes Camunda 8 from earlier Camunda Platform 7 approaches based on Zeebe.

The message is correlated to exactly one of the waiting subscriptions of the same process. Zeebe explicitly defines this cardinality: when multiple subscriptions for the same process match the same message name and correlation key, the message correlates to only one subscription. The other instance keeps waiting and will consume a subsequent published message with the same name and key.

Zeebe handles this case predictably by correlating to one subscription and buffering for timeToLive milliseconds. Duplicate correlation keys in concurrent instances are a design concern -- a best-practice recommendation -- but do not cause runtime crashes or system instability. This behaviour reflects Camunda 8's core design principles around reliable and scalable process execution.

a.text: Incorrect. Per concepts/messages.md:85, Zeebe's cardinality is specified (not implementation-defined): when multiple subscriptions for the same process match the same name + correlation key, the message correlates to only one of them. The other remains open and consumes a subsequent same-key publish. Distinct from cross-process correlation (:87: "When subscriptions are opened for different processes, the message is correlated to all the subscriptions"). Design unique keys per concurrent instance when 1:1 routing matters.

b.text: Incorrect. Messages aren't broadcast like Signals.
